docs.title: MEDVOL
docs.text: Open-source 3D medical image viewer. DICOM & NIfTI. Zero configuration.

docs.title: What is MEDVOL?
docs.text:
MEDVOL is a desktop application for visualising 3D medical imaging data. It opens DICOM and NIfTI volumes, shows them as linked axial/sagittal/coronal slice views, and renders the full volume in 3D using VTK ray-casting.

It was built to be fast, correct, and visually distinct from every other medical viewer — no clinical bloat, no proprietary file format lock-in, no broken aspect ratios.

docs.title: At a Glance
docs.text: Feature Detail 3D rendering VTK vtkFixedPointVolumeRayCastMapper with anatomic transfer functions Multi-planar views Axial, sagittal, coronal — linked crosshair sync on click-drag Voxel spacing Reads PixelSpacing + SliceThickness from DICOM / NIfTI headers Isotropic resampling scipy.ndimage.zoom to cubic voxels before display DICOM formats Single file, multi-frame enhanced, folder series DICOM compression JPEG, JPEG 2000, RLE (via pydicom backend chain) NIfTI .nii and .nii.gz, NIfTI-1 and NIfTI-2 Platform Windows, macOS, Linux

docs.title: Quick Install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/Kareem-Taha-05/medvol
cd medvol
pip install .
medvol

→ See Installation for platform-specific notes and compression backend setup.

docs.title: Design Philosophy
docs.text:
MEDVOL makes three deliberate choices that most medical viewers don't:

1. Isotropic resampling is not optional. CT scans typically have 0.5mm in-plane pixels but 3mm slice spacing. Every viewer that ignores this makes bones look like pancakes. MEDVOL reads the actual spacing metadata and resamples before display.

2. Decompression should be invisible. The DICOM standard allows JPEG, JPEG 2000, and RLE pixel encoding. MEDVOL delegates decompression to pydicom's backend chain — if the right library is installed, compressed files open without any user intervention.

3. The 3D view is the centrepiece. Most viewers treat 3D rendering as an afterthought tucked in a corner. MEDVOL puts the volume render on the left at 60% of the screen width — because that's the most impressive thing the application does.

docs.title: Launch the application
docs.text: medvol

docs.title: Load a NIfTI file
docs.text:
1. Click NIFTI FILE in the command bar
  2. Select a .nii or .nii.gz file
  3. All four panels populate immediately — three 2D slice views and the 3D volume

No NIfTI file?

Download a free brain dataset from OpenNeuro or the IXI Dataset. Any T1-weighted brain MRI works well.

docs.title: Load a DICOM series
docs.text:
1. Click DICOM SERIES
  2. Select the folder containing the .dcm files
  3. MEDVOL reads all files, sorts by InstanceNumber, and resamples to isotropic voxels

docs.title: Load a single DICOM file
docs.text:
1. Click DICOM FILE
  2. Select any .dcm file
  3. MEDVOL checks whether it is:
  4. A multi-frame file → extracts all frames
  5. A single-frame file → searches the folder for sibling slices with the same SeriesInstanceUID
  6. A truly isolated slice → displays it as a 3-slice volume so all views work

docs.title: Navigate slices
docs.text:
Click and drag inside any slice view to move the crosshair. All three planes update in real-time to show the corresponding slice.

Each plane also has a slider — drag it or scroll to step through slices.

docs.title: Adjust brightness and contrast
docs.text:
Use the BRIGHTNESS and CONTRAST sliders in the bottom control bar.

  • Brightness: uniform ±additive offset over the full range
  • Contrast: scale around midpoint — centre is unchanged, extremes clip to black/white

Both sliders return to zero on double-click (standard Qt behaviour).

docs.title: Zoom
docs.text:
1. Click the ZOOM button in the command bar — it turns green (active)
  2. Click ZOOM + then click on a slice view to zoom in on that point
  3. Click ZOOM − then click to zoom out
  4. Click CURSOR to return to crosshair navigation — zoom resets

docs.title: Keyboard reference
docs.text:
Key Action Ctrl+Q Quit

More keyboard shortcuts are planned — see the Roadmap.

docs.title: Programmatic use
docs.text:
from medvol.core.loaders import load_nifti_file, load_dicom_folder

# Load a NIfTI volume
volume = load_nifti_file("brain.nii.gz")
print(volume.shape, volume.dtype)   # (182, 218, 182)  uint8

# Load a DICOM series
volume = load_dicom_folder("/path/to/CT/")
print(volume.shape)   # (N, rows, cols)  uint8

# Brightness/contrast adjustment
from medvol.utils.image_processing import adjust_brightness_contrast
import numpy as np

axial_slice = volume[volume.shape[0] // 2]
adjusted = adjust_brightness_contrast(axial_slice, brightness=20, contrast=40)

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10 or later
  • A display (MEDVOL is a desktop GUI application — it cannot run headlessly)

docs.title: Standard Install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/Kareem-Taha-05/medvol
cd medvol
pip install .

Launch:

medvol

docs.title: With DICOM Compression Support
docs.text:
Most DICOM files from clinical scanners are uncompressed and work out of the box. If your files use JPEG, JPEG 2000, or RLE transfer syntaxes, install the compression extras:

pip install ".[compress]"

This installs:

Package Handles pylibjpeg + pylibjpeg-libjpeg JPEG baseline / JPEG-LS (1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.50/51) python-gdcm JPEG 2000 (1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.90/91) and RLE (1.2.840.10008.1.2.5)

If a file fails to open with a "Cannot decode pixel data" error, installing the above will almost always fix it.

docs.title: Platform Notes
docs.text:
=== "Windows"

```bash
pip install .
# or with compression:
pip install ".[compress]"
```

`python-gdcm` on Windows ships with its own DLLs — no external install needed.
If the VTK wheel fails, check that you are on Python 3.10–3.12 (VTK wheels
are not published for every minor version).

=== "macOS (Apple Silicon)"

VTK's pip wheel may fail on arm64. If it does:

```bash
brew install vtk
pip install PyQt5 pydicom nibabel scipy scikit-image matplotlib
pip install --no-deps .
medvol
```

=== "Linux"

VTK requires `libGL`. If you see an OpenGL error at startup:

```bash
sudo apt install libgl1-mesa-glx libglib2.0-0
pip install .
medvol
```

On headless servers, add a virtual framebuffer:

```bash
sudo apt install xvfb
Xvfb :99 -screen 0 1920x1080x24 &
DISPLAY=:99 medvol
```

docs.title: Virtual Environment (Recommended)
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/Kareem-Taha-05/medvol
cd medvol
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate    # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
pip install .
medvol

docs.title: Development Install
docs.text:
If you want to modify the code and see changes without reinstalling:

pip install -e ".[dev]"
pre-commit install    # optional: auto-format/lint on every commit

docs.title: Verifying the Install
docs.text:
python -c "import medvol; print(medvol.__version__)"
# Expected output: 1.0.0

docs.title: Programmatic use
docs.text:
from medvol.core.loaders import load_nifti_file, load_dicom_folder

# Load a NIfTI volume
volume = load_nifti_file("brain.nii.gz")
print(volume.shape, volume.dtype)   # (182, 218, 182)  uint8

# Load a DICOM series
volume = load_dicom_folder("/path/to/CT/")
print(volume.shape)   # (N, rows, cols)  uint8

# Brightness/contrast adjustment
from medvol.utils.image_processing import adjust_brightness_contrast
import numpy as np

docs.title: utils.image_processing
docs.text:
Pure NumPy image transforms. No Qt, no VTK dependencies.

::: medvol.utils.image_processing options: members: - adjust_brightness_contrast show_source: true

docs.title: core.loaders
docs.text:
File I/O for all supported volume formats.

All public functions return a numpy.ndarray of shape (Z, Y, X) and dtype uint8, with voxels resampled to isotropic spacing.

::: medvol.core.loaders options: members: - load_nifti_file - load_dicom_file - load_dicom_folder show_source: true

docs.title: core.volume_rendering
docs.text:
VTK pipeline: imports a numpy array, applies transfer functions, sets up three-point lighting, and calls Render().

::: medvol.core.volume_rendering options: members: - render_volume show_source: true
